1.1: The Song of Solomon's place in the canon was debated in rabbinic Judaism (Rabbi Akiva defended it: 'all the ages are not worth the day on which the Song of Songs was given to Israel; for all the writings are holy, but the Song of Songs is the Holy of Holies'); it was included in the Hebrew canon and subsequently in the Christian canon. The bride-bridegroom image is the OT's primary metaphor for the YHWH-Israel covenant relationship: Hosea (chs. 1-3) uses the marriage metaphor for covenant and its violation; Isaiah 54:5 calls YHWH Israel's husband; Jeremiah 2:2 recalls the honeymoon period of the wilderness. The NT develops the bridegroom imagery specifically for Jesus (Mark 2:20: the bridegroom is taken away; John 3:29: the friend of the bridegroom rejoices; Eph 5:25-32: husbands love your wives as Christ loved the church; Rev 19:7: the marriage of the Lamb has come).
1.2: The Song opens with an expression of longing: 'Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth.' In the ancient near east, the kiss was a greeting between social equals and an act of intimacy between lovers; kissing the mouth was distinguished from kissing the cheek or hand. The comparison to wine (*yayin*) sets the lover above the standard social pleasure — wine was the prestige beverage at banquets and celebrations, associated with joy and abundance (Ps 104:15). ANE parallels: Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Harris 500) uses identical comparative language — 'your love is sweeter to me than wine.'
1.3: Fragrant oils (*shemen*) were luxury imports in the ancient world — olive oil infused with myrrh, nard, or cinnamon. The king's anointing oil (Exod 30:23-25) and the perfume of the high priest's garments were the supreme aromatic standards. The comparison 'your name is like perfume poured out' reflects the ANE understanding of the personal name as carrier of identity and reputation: to speak a name releases its essence, as pouring perfume releases its scent. 'No wonder the young women love you' — the broader social recognition of the beloved's attractiveness confirms the speaker's taste.
1.4: 'The king has brought me into his chambers' — the royal chambers (*chadarav*) were the innermost rooms of the palace, accessible only by invitation. The harem context of the ancient near eastern court meant that a woman brought to the king's chambers was entering a world of highly stratified privilege. The chorus of 'daughters of Jerusalem' responds with the celebration of the community: 'we will rejoice in you.' The speaker's confidence ('rightly do they love you') reflects the honor-shame social dynamic: the beloved's desirability is socially validated, not merely personal.
1.5: The Shulamite's 'I am dark and lovely' (*shchorah ani venava*) confronts the social reality of skin color in ancient Israel. Dark skin was associated with outdoor labor (field work, shepherding) and therefore with lower social status; lighter skin was the mark of indoor aristocratic women. The comparison to 'tents of Kedar' — Kedar was a nomadic tribal federation of northwestern Arabia, their tents woven from dark goat hair — acknowledges the darkness while the parallel with 'draperies of Solomon' asserts her elegance. She is dark like a nomad's tent but beautiful like a royal curtain.
1.6: 'My brothers were angry with me, made me keeper of the vineyards' — the brothers functioned as guardians of a woman's honor in ancient Israelite society (cf. the brothers' role in Gen 34 regarding Dinah; 2 Sam 13 regarding Tamar and Absalom). Their anger suggests a family conflict that resulted in the woman being assigned outdoor vineyard labor as punishment or consequence — explaining her dark skin. The closing 'my own vineyard I have not tended' introduces the vineyard as a double entendre: the literal vineyard she managed and her own person/beauty, which she had no time to cultivate for herself.
1.7: 'Tell me, you whom I love, where you pasture your flock' — the pastoral setting locates the beloved as a shepherd. In the ancient near east, kings were regularly called 'shepherd' of their people (the Sumerian title *sipa* and Akkadian *re'um* both mean shepherd-king; YHWH is Israel's shepherd in Ps 23). Whether the figure is literally a shepherd or a king described in pastoral terms, the question is intimate: she wants to find him at his resting place at the heat of the day (midday rest was customary for flocks to avoid the heat), so she does not have to wander among other shepherds' flocks.
1.8: The response — 'follow the tracks of the flock and pasture your young goats near the shepherds' tents' — gives practical pastoral guidance. The image of the woman following the goats to find the beloved evokes the idealized wilderness period of Israel's courtship with YHWH: Jeremiah 2:2 recalls Israel following YHWH through the wilderness, 'a land not sown,' as the honeymoon of the covenant relationship. The pastoral setting connects the Song's love to Israel's foundational narrative of divine-human intimacy.
1.9: 'I compare you to a mare harnessed to Pharaoh's chariots' — a striking and culturally specific comparison. Egyptian records of chariot warfare (e.g., the battle of Kadesh) document the tactic of releasing a mare among enemy stallions to disrupt the chariot horses; the single mare could scatter an entire formation of war stallions. In this sense, the comparison means: your beauty alone disrupts and captivates all other men. Alternatively, the mare (*susati*) harnessed to Pharaoh's chariot was itself the finest, highest-status horse — the comparison praises her as the most outstanding of her kind.
1.10: Jewelry adorning the cheeks and neck — strings of beads (*charutzim*) and ornamental plaques — was common in the ancient near east as shown in Egyptian and Levantine art. The archaeological record (e.g., Megiddo ivories) shows elaborately adorned women of the aristocratic class with multiple necklace strands and cheek ornaments. The beloved's jewelry marks her status and beauty; the lover's gaze inventory of jewelry precedes his offer to add more.
1.11: 'We will make for you gold ornaments with silver' — the royal jewelers and craftsmen (*na'aseh*: 'we will make') promise to add to the beloved's adornment. The combination of gold (*zahav*) and silver (*kesef*) echoes the luxury materials of the tabernacle and temple; in the Song's context they mark the highest craftsmanship. The workshop of the king's goldsmiths is implicitly in the background.
1.12: 'While the king reclined at his table' — the *mesev* (reclining at a table) reflects the banquet posture of the ancient near eastern elite, predating the Hellenistic *symposion* but sharing its social form: the host reclining on a couch while guests and entertainment are arranged around. The beloved's perfume (*nard*, an aromatic root imported from India or the Himalayan region) spreads through the room — its fragrance is her gift to the gathering, a social act of sensory presence.
1.13: Myrrh (*mor*) was a costly resin imported from Arabia and the Horn of Africa; it was an ingredient in the holy anointing oil (Exod 30:23), used in burial preparations (John 19:39-40), and worn as a personal perfume. The sachet worn between the breasts was a known ANE custom — small sealed bags of aromatic material worn as jewelry and body perfume. The myrrh's dual association with anointing and death gives the image an undertow of solemnity beneath the erotic surface.
1.14: En-gedi was an oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea — in the midst of arid wilderness, it supported lush subtropical vegetation fed by fresh springs. Henna (*kopher*) blossoms grew at En-gedi and were used to color fingernails and hair a rich reddish color; they are fragrant white or cream-colored flowers. David hid at En-gedi when fleeing Saul (1 Sam 24:1), making it a place associated with refuge and concealment — the beloved is the beloved's place of refuge.
1.15: 'Your eyes are like doves' — the *wasf* (descriptive praise poem) begins. The dove (*yona*) was associated with purity, gentleness, and the cooing call of longing in Israelite culture (Song 2:12; Ps 74:19; Nah 2:7 uses dove imagery for mourning). The dove's eyes were considered particularly beautiful — round, soft, dark, and expressive. This is the opening of the extended love-description pattern that runs through the Song.
1.16: 'Our bed is a bower of green' — the outdoor bedroom of the lovers; the *arsa* (bed/couch) made of greenery reflects the idealized union in nature. The garden setting connects to the Eden motif: the first man and woman in the garden are the prototype for the lovers of the Song. The lushness of the natural setting — green, fragrant, sheltered — evokes the pre-fall Paradise where human love and nature were in harmony.
1.17: 'Beams of cedar, rafters of cypress' — cedar (*erez*) from Lebanon and cypress (*berot*) were the luxury building materials of the ancient world; Solomon built both the temple and his palace using Lebanese cedar (1 Kgs 6:14-18; 7:2-3). The lovers' natural canopy of trees is described with the same royal building vocabulary, suggesting that their love-bower is as magnificent as the finest human architecture — the forest provides a temple-like dwelling.

7.1: The second full wasf (Arabic: descriptive praise poem) of the Song, unique in running from feet to head — an ascending rather than descending survey. This reversal of the usual head-to-foot sequence (used in 4:1-7) is unparalleled in the surviving ANE love poetry corpus and may signal a new vantage point: the beloved is viewed in the context of a processional dance (machola ha-machanayim, the dance of the two camps, 6:13). The title hassulammit — the only proper name for the beloved in the Song — derives most likely from Shlomo (Solomon) as its feminine counterpart, or possibly from shalom (peace/wholeness), making her 'the peaceable one.' The thighs compared to jewels worked by a craftsman: in the ancient Near East, skilled craftsmen (ben-aman, son of a master, a technical guild title) produced ornamental metalwork worn as identity markers. The quality of sandals was a social indicator: only slaves went unshod (cf. the sandal-binding idiom in John 1:27; Mark 1:7), making sandaled feet a marker of free status and honor.
7.2: The shorer (H8326, navel/umbilical region, a hapax legomenon appearing only here) contributes to the challenge of this verse: it is either the navel itself or the curve of the abdomen. The comparison to an 'aggan (H101, a mixing bowl for wine) — round and never lacking mixed wine — combines the visual image of a curved vessel with the sensory image of abundance. The belly like a heap of wheat (aremah hittim) surrounded by lilies is a landscape-to-body metaphor that appears frequently in Egyptian love poetry: the Papyrus Chester Beatty I (13th century BCE) parallels descriptions of the beloved's body using agricultural abundance imagery. The heap of wheat specifically recalls the threshing floor, the site of harvest abundance and — in biblical narrative — significant social encounters (Ruth 3; cf. also Hosea 9:1). The lily-bordered wheat image combines harvest plenty with garden delicacy in a deliberately rich sensory compound.
7.3: The twin-fawn simile for the breasts repeats 4:5 nearly verbatim: shenei shadayikh kishenei ofarim te'omei tseviyah (your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle). The near-verbatim repetition within a single book is a recognized feature of ancient Near Eastern poetry — not a scribal error but a structural device of deliberate echo. The twin gazelle image is attested in Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Harris 500, Song 2A) and in Ugaritic poetry for pairing of equals. Gazelle fawns were prized in the ancient Near East for their softness and the matching perfection of a twinned pair; the image captures both sensory gentleness and perfect symmetry. The deliberate internal echo between 4:5 and 7:3 creates a chiastic inclusio in the Song's central section, bracketing the wasf passages.
7.4: The geographical catalogue in 7:4 anchors the poem in specific, historically significant landscapes: Heshbon was the former Amorite royal city of King Sihon (Num 21:25-34), conquered by Israel in the Transjordanian campaign; the city and its pools are mentioned in Isaiah 16:8-9 as well. The pools of Heshbon (breichot be-cheshbon) at the gate of bat-rabbim (daughter of the multitude or daughter of many, H1337) were likely well-known irrigation features in the Transjordanian landscape. Damascus, the ancient capital of Aram/Syria, appears as a comparator for the nose — the prominent tower of Damascus toward Lebanon (migdal levanon tsofeh penei dammaseq) would have been recognizable to the audience as a landmark of the northern horizon. The ivory tower (migdal ha-shen) for the neck is the only ivory tower reference in the Hebrew Bible; ivory was an elite luxury material in the ancient Near East, associated with royal courts (1 Kings 22:39: Ahab's ivory house; Ps 45:8: ivory palaces).
7.5: Ha-karmel (H3760) is both the specific place name (Mount Carmel, the prominent coastal promontory running into the Mediterranean at modern Haifa) and the common noun meaning a cultivated garden-land or fertile field. Carmel's distinctive feature in the landscape was its dense forests and orchards contrasting with the surrounding terrain — Isaiah 35:2 uses Carmel as an image of exceptional beauty and abundance. The hair like purple (velachur arganah) is the color of royalty — Tyrian purple, the most expensive dye of the ancient world, extracted from Murex sea snails along the Phoenician coast. The final clause — melekh asur ba-rehatim (the king is held/captivated in the flowing locks) — uses rahut (H7298, flowing channels/troughs or possibly streaming locks) to suggest that the beloved's hair has entrapped the king as channels capture flowing water. The image of hair as a net/trap for the beloved appears in Egyptian love poetry and in Prov 7:21 (seductress).
7.6: The direct-address appreciation — mah-yafet umah-na'amt ahavah bata'anugim (how fair and how pleasant you are, O love, among delights) — steps back from the anatomical inventory of the wasf to offer the holistic impression. Ta'anug (H8588, delight, pleasantness, luxury) is used of royal luxury in Micah 1:16 and of the delights of Qohelet's life project in Ecclesiastes 2:8. The plural bata'anugim (among/with all your delights) may be a genitive of attendant characteristic: love itself as the supreme delight. In Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Chester Beatty I), the holistic appreciation of the beloved — stepping back from the detailed description to exclaim about the whole — is a recognized formal transition in the wasf genre: detailed description followed by comprehensive appreciation is the standard pattern.
7.7: The date palm (tamar, H8558) was the economically dominant tree of the ancient Near East — the date palm was a symbol of Jericho (city of palms, Deut 34:3), of Mesopotamian abundance in Babylonian texts, and of beauty in Egyptian art. Palm trees were used as architectural motifs in Solomon's temple (1 Kings 6:29, 32, 35) and in Ezekiel's vision of the restored temple (Ezek 40:16-31). In the ancient Near East, the date palm was one of the tallest, most graceful, and most productive trees in the agricultural landscape; a woman's stature compared to a date palm invokes all of these associations — height, grace, and fruitfulness. The breasts compared to date clusters (eshkolot, H811, clusters of grapes or dates) continues the harvest-abundance vocabulary that characterizes this wasf.
7.8: The declaration that the bridegroom will ascend the palm tree (e'eleh ba-tamar, I will ascend by the palm tree) describes an actual practice: date palm climbing for harvest was performed by skilled workers in the ancient Near East, climbing with a rope-and-foot technique to reach the date clusters at the crown. The date harvest was a major agricultural and commercial event — dates were pressed into blocks for trade, fermented into date wine, and stored as a caloric staple. The clusters/boughs (sansinav, H5577, its branches or fruiting stalks, a hapax in this form) were the primary commercial product. The comparison of the beloved's breath to apples (tapuchim, H8598, apples or quince) continues the orchard-garden sensory vocabulary of the Song's consistent landscape, and the comparison of her mouth to the best wine sets up the transition to v9b where the beloved's own voice takes over.
7.9: The mid-verse voice transition — the beloved takes up the speech in mid-sentence — is a distinctive feature of the Song's use of multiple voices and is noted by virtually all commentators. Holekh ledodi lemeyasharim dovev siftei yeshenim (going down smoothly for my beloved, flowing over the lips of those who are asleep) — the wine-flowing image for speech that flows sweetly and easily, even rousing the sleeping. The yeshenim (H3463, those who are asleep/sleeping ones) in connection with wine has been interpreted as the lips of the sleeping beloved murmuring to the bridegroom even in sleep, or as wine that revives even sleepers. Egyptian love poetry uses the image of wine-smooth speech as a standard compliment for the beloved's voice, connecting the sensory pleasures of speech and wine.
7.10: Teshuqah (H8669, desire, longing) is one of the most theologically significant words in the Song. It appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible: Genesis 3:16 (the post-fall curse on the woman: 'your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you'), Genesis 4:7 (sin crouching at the door with desire for Cain), and here in 7:10. The Genesisoccurrences are both associated with distorted desire — the post-fall gender hierarchy and the desire of sin for the sinner. Song 7:10 — ani ledodi ve'alai teshuqato (I am my beloved's and his desire is for me) — represents the positive redemption of the term: desire that is mutual, reciprocal, and uncoerced. This is the only occurrence of teshuqah in the Bible where the desire is straightforwardly positive and restored, suggesting that the Song participates in the redemption of what the fall corrupted in Genesis 3.
7.11: The invitation to go out into the fields and lodge in the villages (kefar, H3723, villages/hamlets — the plural of the very common agricultural settlement unit) is the beloved's initiative — she invites the bridegroom into her pastoral world. The rural pastoral setting contrasts with the urban tensions in 3:1-5 and 5:7 (city watchmen, urban night-search scenes). In the ancient Near East, the agricultural village was the basic social unit; the invitation to lodge in the village rather than the city palace suggests an egalitarian intimacy outside the formal structures of royal court life. Egyptian love poetry frequently uses the garden and the rural estate as the setting for erotic encounter, contrasting the freedom of the open countryside with the formal constraints of the house/city.
7.12: The spring vineyard inspection — nistrah hagefen pitach hasemadar heniczu ha-rimmonim (whether the vine has budded, whether the tender grape blossom has opened, whether the pomegranates have bloomed) — repeats the spring-survey vocabulary of 2:13 almost exactly (hagefanim semdar natenu reach, the vines in blossom give off fragrance; the pomegranates in bloom). The deliberate verbal echo creates a seasonal cycle: what was described as a sign of spring's arrival (2:13) is now proposed as an occasion for the beloved to give her love. The vineyard as the setting for erotic encounter was a recognized literary convention in Egyptian love poetry (Papyrus Harris 500, Song 4: 'come, let us go to the field and make our bed in the garden'); the vineyard also carries its covenant connotations in the OT (Isa 5:1-7 uses the vineyard for YHWH and Israel).
7.13: Duda'im (H1736, mandrakes) are the Mandragora officinarum, a plant with fleshy fruit that grows in the Mediterranean region and has been associated with fertility and erotic stimulation in ancient Near Eastern popular belief. Mandrakes appear in Genesis 30:14-16, where Reuben finds them in the field during the wheat harvest and Rachel trades Jacob's company for Leah's mandrakes — a text that explicitly connects mandrakes with fertility desire. In ANE popular medicine, mandrakes were considered aphrodisiacs and fertility enhancers; the Akkadian term nam-tar-gira (love plant) may be a parallel. The 'new and old' stored fruits (chadashim gam-yeshanim tsafanti lecha, new things as well as old things I have stored up for you) suggests accumulated gifts across seasons — a long-term investment of love expressed in gathered fruit. The pattern of both new and old echoes the wisdom teacher's treasure in Matthew 13:52.

8.1: The wish — mi yittencha ke'ach li (who will give you to me as a brother) — uses the idiom mi yitten (if only! would that!) characteristic of Hebrew wishes for the impossible or deeply desired (cf. Job 14:13: 'Oh that you would hide me in Sheol'; Jer 9:1: 'Oh that my head were waters'). The underlying social constraint the wish expresses: in the ancient Near East, brothers and sisters could display physical affection publicly (the kiss of greeting was socially sanctioned within the family), but romantic couples showing public affection invited censure. Deuteronomy 22:28-29 and related texts indicate that unmarried couples meeting privately faced social and legal scrutiny. The wish to be treated as a brother is a wish for the social freedom of kinship affection within a romantic relationship — the longing for a covenant that would make what is now constrained socially legitimate and free.
8.2: The mother's house (beit immi, H517, my mother's house) appears at 3:4 also as the legitimate social container for love — the beloved would bring the bridegroom there, suggesting that the mother's household was the proper venue for courtship within social convention. The mother as the one who teaches (telammedeni, who would instruct/teach me) is the female wisdom figure of the household — in Proverbs, the mother's teaching is explicitly part of the formation of the young (Prov 1:8: 'hear your father's instruction and do not forsake your mother's teaching'). The spiced wine and pomegranate juice (mine-sheqari min-asis rimmonei) are the luxuries of abundance in the Song's vocabulary of sensory pleasure — wine spiced with aromatics and pressed pomegranate juice were elite beverages in the ancient Near East, the products of craft-level processing of agricultural goods.
8.3: The left-hand/right-hand embrace — semalo tachat roshi ve-yamino techabbakeni (his left hand under my head and his right hand embraces me) — repeats 2:6 verbatim. In ancient Near Eastern love poetry, the reclining embrace with one hand as a pillow was a recognized posture of intimacy; Egyptian tomb paintings and iconography show similar embrace postures. The right hand as the arm of action and the left as the support: in ancient Israelite culture, the right hand was the dominant hand for action, protection, and blessing (Gen 48:13-14; Ps 110:1), while the left hand's supportive role under the head creates the image of complete envelopment. The verbatim repetition of 2:6 here is an example of the Song's structural use of refrain-like repetition across the book's arc.
8.4: The daughters of Jerusalem adjuration (hishba'ti etkem benot yerushalayim, I adjure you O daughters of Jerusalem) appears for the fourth and final time (2:7, 3:5, 8:4). The earlier two instances invoked 'by the gazelles or the does of the field' — here the animal oath formula is absent, which may signal the maturation of the relationship: the love that needed the protective adjuration is now so firmly established that the oath can stand without the wilderness-animal invocation. The daughters of Jerusalem are the urban audience, the social chorus of the Song who serve as foils for the rural beloved. The content of the adjuration — mah-ta'iru umah-te'oreru et-ha'ahavah ad shetech'pats (why do you stir up or awaken love until it pleases) — consistently warns against forced or premature love. The refrain functions as a wisdom warning embedded in the love poem: love must arise in its own time.
8.5: The wilderness-procession image (mi zo't olah min-hamidbar mitrapeket al-dodah, who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved?) repeats the form of 3:6's rhetorical question, but now the figure is identified by her relationship: she is leaning on her beloved, whereas 3:6 was an unidentified pillar of smoke. The apple tree (tappuach, H8598) where the mother aroused her beloved and gave birth — the apple or quince tree appears as a sensory pleasure in 2:3 (sitting under his shadow with great delight) and 2:5 (sustain me with apples). The apple tree as the site of both erotic arousal and birth creates a complex of fertility and love imagery. The wilderness-to-apple-tree movement traces the journey from undomesticated desire to rooted, fruitful intimacy: the beloved comes up from the wilderness and arrives at the cultivated garden tree where the covenant of love was first rooted.
8.6: The seal passage (8:6-7) is the theological and poetic climax of the Song. Seals in the ancient Near East were the primary instruments of personal identity and legal authority: cylinder seals (worn around the neck on a cord) or stamp seals (worn as a ring or pendant on the arm) were used to authenticate documents and ownership. To be a seal on someone's heart or arm was to be their most intimate possession and primary identification mark — Jeremiah 22:24 uses the signet ring on the right hand as an image of intimacy with God (though Coniah's signet would be removed). The comparison of love to death (ki azza kamavet ahavah, for love is strong as death) and jealousy to Sheol (qasha kishol qina'ah, jealousy/passion is fierce as Sheol) uses the two most powerful and inescapable forces in human experience as comparators. Death and Sheol could not be resisted in the ancient Near Eastern worldview; to say love is as strong as these is to say it is as absolute and inescapable. Shalhevetyah — the very flame of the LORD — is a rare compound embedding the divine name (YH = abbreviated YHWH) into the word for flame: the flame of divine love burning in the relationship.
8.7: The flood-water imagery (mayim rabbim lo yukelu lechabbot et-ha'ahavah, many waters are not able to quench the love; neharot, rivers, cannot drown it) draws on the ANE mythological tradition of the primordial chaos waters as the most threatening force against life and order. In Babylonian cosmology, the primordial Tiamat (chaos-water) was the enemy of the ordered cosmos; in Canaanite mythology, Yam (sea) was the rival of Baal for cosmic dominance. The prophetic literature uses the flood imagery for overwhelming forces of devastation (Isa 8:7-8: the Assyrian flood; Ps 93:3-4: the floods lifting their voices against YHWH). Love that the flood-waters cannot quench is love that withstands the maximum of cosmic threat. The social dimension of v7b — even if a man offered all his house's wealth for love, he would be utterly despised (buz yevuzu lo) — asserts love's non-commercial nature: love cannot be purchased, and the attempt to purchase it invites contempt. Wealth and love exist in incommensurable categories.
8.8: The brothers' council scene (8:8-9) introduces the brothers as guardians of their sister's honor — a social institution attested throughout the ancient Near East and biblical narrative. In ancient Israelite law and custom, brothers were the primary protectors of unmarried sisters' sexual honor: Genesis 34 (Dinah's brothers avenging her violation); 2 Samuel 13 (Absalom avenging Tamar); Leviticus 21:3 (the priest may defile himself for an unmarried virgin sister). The little sister (achot lanu qetannah, we have a little sister) who has no breasts yet — a younger sibling not yet at marriageable age — represents the brothers' future social responsibility: they must determine how to prepare for the day when she will be sought in marriage (be-yom sheyedubar bah, in the day she is spoken of/negotiated for). The question 'what shall we do for our sister?' reflects the genuine social deliberation about how to protect and honorably present a younger sibling when the time comes.
8.9: The wall/door alternatives present two possible characters for the younger sister: chomah (H2346, wall) as the woman who maintains firm sexual boundaries, and delet (H1817, door) as the woman who is permissive or easily opened. If she is a wall — maintaining honor — the brothers will build upon her a tirat kesef (H2918 + H3701, a turret/battlement of silver): they will add to and honor her fortifications, adorning her honor. If she is a door — sexually permissive — they will enclose her with boards of cedar (natsur aleha luach erez), sealing her off to prevent further access. The cedar board-enclosure represents not punishment but protection: cedar was the most prestigious building material (the temple's cedar paneling, 1 Kings 6:15-16), so enclosing a door with cedar turns it into a wall of the most valuable material. Both responses, wall and door, end in the brothers' protective action, not condemnation.
8.10: The beloved's self-identification — ani chomah u-shadai kamigdalot (I am a wall and my breasts are like towers) — picks up the brothers' wall/door binary and answers with the wall: she has been the wall throughout. Az hayiti be'einav kemotse'et shalom (thus I became in his eyes as one who finds peace/favor): the word shalom here plays against the name Solomon (shlomoh), creating a word-level pun where the woman's wholeness/peace is the answer to the king's own name. Shalom as the state of wholeness, well-being, and covenant completion is the beloved's self-description after maintaining integrity: she did not yield prematurely, and in finding peace in the bridegroom's eyes she has achieved the wholeness that the Song has been moving toward throughout. In the allegorical reading, Israel's covenant faithfulness to YHWH produces the shalom that is the covenant's promise (Num 6:26).
8.11: Solomon's vineyard at Ba'al Hamon (H1174, lord of abundance or lord of the multitude) is an unidentified location — no archaeological site has been securely identified with this name, which suggests either a legendary place or a poetic toponym meaning 'place of great abundance.' The vineyard-keeper arrangement (natan et-ha-kerem la-notreim, he gave the vineyard to the keepers) for a thousand pieces of silver each reflects actual ancient Near Eastern viticulture management: large vineyards were commonly managed through tenant-keeper arrangements where the owner received a fixed portion of the harvest value. Isaiah 7:23 uses the thousand-silver-vine as an image of the most valuable possible vineyard. The economic framework of vineyard value — royal vineyard worth thousands of silver per keeper — sets up the beloved's contrast in v12.
8.12: The climax of the vineyard imagery (karmi shelli lefanai, my own vineyard is before me, mine) — the emphatic shelli (mine, my own, from the preposition shel + first person) resolves the vine-narrative that opened the Song. In 1:6, the beloved said 'my own vineyard I have not kept' — she was forced to tend her brothers' vineyards at the expense of her own. Now at the Song's close she asserts full possession: her vineyard is completely her own and is before her (intact, unharvested, fully hers to offer). The thousand to Solomon and the two hundred to the keepers continues the economic framework of v11 but with a different referent: now the beloved herself distributes the vineyard's value according to her own sovereignty, with the two hundred going to 'those who keep the fruit.' In the allegorical reading, Israel's vineyard is YHWH's covenant relationship; the beloved keeping her own vineyard represents covenant faithfulness returned to its proper owner.
8.13: The bridegroom's call to the garden-dwelling beloved — hayyoshevet ba-gannim chaverim maqshivim le-qolech hashme'ini (O you who dwell in the gardens, the companions are listening for your voice — let me hear it) — introduces the community of companions (chaverim, H2270, friends/companions) who are also listening for the beloved's voice. This communal dimension — that the beloved's speech is sought not only by the bridegroom but by an audience of companions — mirrors the Song's own social performance context: the poem was sung in community. In Jewish tradition, the Song was sung at weddings and festival celebrations; the companions listening for the beloved's voice are the wedding guests, the community witnessing the love relationship. The beloved who dwells in gardens (yoshevet ba-gannim) has achieved the garden-paradise that was the implicit goal of the Song's pastoral imagery throughout.
8.14: The Song's final verse — berach dodi vedameh lekha litsvi o le'ofer ha'ayyalim al-harei vesamim (flee, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices) — returns to the gazelle/stag image of 2:9, 2:17, and 4:6, completing the Song's circular structure. The verb berach (flee, H1272) is the same verb used in 2:17 and 4:6 for the stag's departure at dawn — suggesting not a permanent farewell but the movement that precedes a return: the beloved sends the bridegroom out into the mountains of spices (harei vesamim), the fragrant landscape where he will be most himself, with the implicit invitation to return. The Song ends not with consummation described but with anticipation extended — a structural feature consistent with the Song's overall movement through longing, encounter, separation, and renewed longing. This ending is eschatologically charged: the love celebrated throughout the Song is not yet fully realized; its completion lies ahead, in the same direction as the stag bounding over the mountains.

2.1: 'I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys' — the *chavatzelet hasharon* is likely not the modern rose but a bulb flower: narcissus, crocus, or perhaps the autumn crocus that blooms on the coastal plain of Sharon. The Sharon (*hasharon*) was the fertile coastal plain between Jaffa and Mount Carmel, known for its abundant wildflowers. The self-description is modest — a wildflower, not a cultivated garden specimen — a contrast to the elaborate descriptions the beloved will apply to her.
2.2: 'Like a lily among thorns' — the beloved's response immediately elevates her self-deprecating comparison. The sharp contrast between the lily and the surrounding thorns makes her stand out as extraordinary against the ordinary. In ANE love poetry, such escalating comparisons were conventional: the woman compares herself modestly; the man responds by raising the comparison. The daughters (other young women) are the 'thorns' by contrast — not an insult but a poetic device to establish her unique beauty.
2.3: 'Like an apple tree among the trees of the forest' — the *tapuach* (possibly apricot or quince rather than modern apple; quince was the most fragrant) is distinguished from the wild forest trees by its fruit and its shade. Sitting in shade was a practical and precious comfort in the near east where heat was a real threat. The shade-giver (*tzel*) appears also in Ps 121:5 (YHWH is your shade) and Isa 49:2 (hidden in the shadow of his hand). The image of rest under the beloved's shade is both sensory and protective.
2.4: 'He brought me to the banquet hall' — literally 'the house of wine' (*beit haiayin*), the dining room of the palace or estate. The banner (*degel*) was a military standard carried before troops to identify and rally them — each tribe of Israel had a banner (Num 2). The beloved's banner over her is 'love': he has publicly identified her as his own, placed her under his protection and claim. The honor-shame social structure of the ancient near east made this public claiming essential — she is under his banner, socially protected.
2.5: 'Strengthen me with raisins, revive me with apples' — *ashishot* (raisin cakes or pressed grape clusters) were a concentrated energy food and also used as offering gifts (Hos 3:1 associates them with Baal worship, suggesting their festive associations). Lovesickness (*halat ahavah*, 'sick with love') was a recognized condition in ANE literature — Egyptian love poetry describes the physical symptoms of love's absence (pallor, loss of appetite, racing heart). The need for food-revival frames love as an overwhelming physical experience.
2.6: 'His left arm under my head, his right arm holds me close' — the protective embrace of the sleeping couple; the left arm as a pillow, the right arm as an encircling hold. This posture of mutual rest and protection recurs as a refrain in the Song (8:3 repeats it). The arm under the head was also a posture depicted in Egyptian love imagery.
2.7: 'I charge you by the gazelles and the does of the field: do not rouse or awaken love until it pleases' — a liturgical-style oath formula (*hishbati* = I adjure you) using wild animals as witnesses. The gazelle (*tzvi*) and doe (*ayalah*) were associated with swiftness, grace, and the wild freedom of nature; they invoke the untamed world as the context for this charge. The threefold refrain of this charge (2:7; 3:5; 8:4) functions as the Song's primary warning: love, once awakened, is powerful and must not be disturbed; its timing is not subject to human management.
2.8: 'Listen — my beloved! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains' — the lover's arrival is announced with the same urgency of a royal messenger. The leaping (*medaleig*) and bounding (*meqaphetz*) across mountains and hills echoes Psalm 18:33 (he makes my feet like deer's feet) and Habakkuk 3:19. The gazelle/stag comparison applied to the lover marks him as swift, sure-footed, and graceful — qualities valued in both warriors and noblemen in ANE culture.
2.9: 'Standing behind our wall, peering through the windows' — the architecture of separation: the beloved is outside, looking in through latticed windows (*charakkim*). Ancient near eastern houses were built with inner courtyards and latticed windows facing inward or outward; the lattice (*eshnav*) provided ventilation and partial visibility without full exposure. The peering beloved is caught between outside and inside, desiring entry but not yet admitted — the scene of longing through separation.
2.10: 'Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come with me' — the invitation formula (*qumi lakh*) is a summons to movement and departure. The double address 'my love' (*rayati*) and 'my beautiful one' (*yafati*) combines relational affection with aesthetic praise; both terms appear repeatedly throughout the Song as the beloved's primary titles. The call to 'come' (*lekhi lakh*) echoes the divine call to Abraham ('go forth' uses the same reflexive *lekh-lekha* construction, Gen 12:1) — a summons that changes the course of a life.
2.11: 'For the winter is past; the rains are over and gone' — the Palestinian agricultural year divided into the dry season (summer, April-October) and the rainy season (winter, October-March). The end of the rains marked the beginning of the agricultural year's productive phase: plowing, planting, and the appearance of new growth. Spring was the season of Passover and the feast of firstfruits — associated with liberation (the Exodus) and new creation. The beloved's invitation to come is set in the context of renewal and release.
2.12: 'Flowers appear all across the land; the season of singing has arrived; the voice of the turtledove is heard' — the three signs of spring in Israel: wildflower bloom, the return of the turtledove (*tor*), and the season of song (*zmir*, also the word for pruning — a season both of pruning vines and of singing). The turtledove was a migratory bird (Jer 8:7) whose spring arrival signaled the season's change; it was also the offering of the poor at the temple (Lev 5:7; 12:8; Luke 2:24), giving it a cultic resonance.
2.13: 'The fig tree is ripening its early fruit; the blossoming vines spread their fragrance' — the fig (*teenah*) produced an early crop of small figs (*paggim*, green figs) before the main harvest; their ripening indicated late spring. The blossoming vineyard (*gefen*, vine; *semadar*, blossom) had a distinctive sweet fragrance as the flowers opened before setting fruit. The beloved's invitation to 'come' is framed by the agricultural signs of the land at its most fruitful moment — an invitation into abundance.
2.14: 'My dove, hidden in the clefts of the rock, in the sheltered crevice of the cliff' — rock clefts were the natural hiding places of doves in the ancient near east; the dove's safety was in its inaccessibility. The image of the beloved hidden and beautiful in the rock echoes Exodus 33:21-23 where YHWH places Moses in the cleft of the rock to shield him from the divine glory while allowing a partial view. The beloved, like Moses in the cleft, is both protected and desirous of fuller presence; the request is to see and hear her.
2.15: 'Catch the little foxes that ruin the vineyards — our vineyards are in bloom' — the foxes (*shualim*; likely jackals or young foxes) that damage vineyards were a real agricultural threat, noted in ANE agricultural texts. The vineyard 'in bloom' (*semadar*) is at its most vulnerable stage: the blossoms must set fruit before any damage ruins the crop. Interpretively, the little foxes may represent the small threats and distractions that endanger a relationship in its early, tender phase — the 'in bloom' stage of love that needs protection.
2.16: 'My beloved is mine and I am his; he grazes among the lilies' — the refrain of mutual possession (*dodi li va'ani lo*) is the Song's central declaration, restated three times in slightly varying forms (6:3; 7:10). It reflects the covenant language of YHWH and Israel: 'I will be your God, and you will be my people' (Jer 30:22; Ezek 36:28). The shepherd imagery ('grazes among the lilies') locates the beloved in the pastoral paradise — not among thorns (contrast 2:2) but in the most beautiful pasture.
2.17: 'Until the day breaks and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved — be like a gazelle on the rugged mountains' — the 'mountains of separation' (*harei bater*; *beter* possibly means 'divided' or 'rugged') is an uncertain location; it may refer to specific hills or simply describe steep, divided terrain. The request is for the beloved to remain through the night and be present until dawn. The dawn (*shachar*) and the disappearing shadows frame a single night: the beloved is asked to stay from darkness until the first light — the most intimate request of the chapter.

3.1: 'At night on my bed I searched for the one I love' — the night search is a literary device common in ANE love poetry; whether this is a literal experience or a dream-vision is debated. Egyptian love poetry uses the same motif: the lover wandering in search of the beloved. The repetition 'I searched for him but did not find him' creates the tension of longing and absence that drives the poem. The bed (*mishkav*) is the natural place of rest and intimacy; the beloved's absence from it is what prompts the search.
3.2: 'I will go about the city, through the streets and squares' — the city (*ir*) as the setting of danger and search; the open squares (*rechovot*) and narrow streets (*shevaqim*) of ancient Jerusalem or an idealized city. A woman alone in the city at night was socially transgressive in ancient near eastern society: women's public movement was restricted by honor-shame conventions that linked female exposure to family honor. The beloved's willingness to violate these conventions for love's sake marks the intensity of her longing.
3.3: 'The city guards found me as they made their rounds' — the *shomerim* (watchmen/guards) were the night security of ancient cities, patrolling the streets to prevent crime and enforce order. Their encounter with the woman alone at night is neutral here (they are asked a question and implicitly answer by her movement); contrast the hostile encounter in 5:7. The question 'have you seen the one I love?' — asking strangers for information about a private beloved — makes her vulnerability public, a transgression of the propriety that would normally keep such a search private.
3.4: 'I had barely passed them when I found the one I love — I held him and would not let him go' — the language *achaztihu* ('I held him/seized him') echoes Genesis 32:26 where Jacob refuses to release the wrestling figure: 'I will not let you go unless you bless me.' The tenacity of the beloved's grip on her lover is cast in the language of the most famous wrestling encounter in the OT — love's grip as persistent as faith's grip. The 'mother's house' (*beit immi*) rather than 'father's house' is unusual; it may reflect the woman's domestic sphere or a matrilocal marriage custom.
3.5: The refrain repeated (cf. 2:7): 'I charge you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and does of the field: do not rouse or awaken love until it pleases.' The context here follows the successful finding and holding; the charge is a kind of warning after the danger of the search — love has been found but its awakening and timing remain precious and not to be disturbed by others. The repetition of the charge after the dream-episode reinforces its force.
3.6: 'Who is this coming up from the desert like a column of smoke?' — the image of a column of smoke (*timrot ashan*) from the wilderness evokes the divine presence in the Exodus: the pillar of cloud by day and fire by night (Exod 13:21-22; Num 14:14). A royal procession emerging from the desert trailing fragrance — myrrh (*mor*), frankincense (*levonah*), and merchants' spices — would have been a dramatic sight. The question 'who is this?' is the same form as the question about Wisdom in Prov 31:10 and about the eschatological figures in Isa 63:1.
3.7: 'The royal litter of Solomon, surrounded by sixty warriors' — the *mittato* (litter/couch of Solomon) served as the conveyance for a royal procession or bridal transport. The sixty *gibbore hagibborum* (mighty men of the mighty) echo David's mighty men — the thirty (*shaloshim*) and other elite warriors of 2 Sam 23. Here sixty — twice thirty — forms a royal bodyguard; they are 'skilled in war, each with sword at his side, ready for danger in the night.'
3.8: 'All of them carry swords, skilled in battle, each with sword at side, ready for danger in the night' — the *pachad ballailot* (dread of the night) was a real security concern for ancient processions; bandit attacks on traveling parties were a constant threat in the ancient near east. The armed escort of the royal litter represents the intersection of love and power: the most intimate transport is guarded by the most capable soldiers. The *gibbor* (mighty man) as escort figure appears in royal and military contexts throughout the OT.
3.9: 'King Solomon made his royal litter from Lebanese cedar' — the *apiryon* (bridal carriage or palanquin; a loanword possibly from Sanskrit *paryanka* via Persian) was a covered carrying-chair used for royal and bridal transport. Lebanese cedar (*erez ha'levanon*) was the finest available building material (1 Kgs 5-7; Ps 92:12); its use for the litter indicates the highest luxury. The choice of cedar connects the beloved's bridal transport to the materials of the temple — love housed in temple-grade material.
3.10: 'Its posts are silver, its back of gold, its seat purple, its interior lovingly lined by the daughters of Jerusalem' — the materials echo the tabernacle and temple furnishings: silver (*kesef*) posts, gold (*zahav*) back, purple (*argaman*) seat — all are exact descriptions of tabernacle materials (Exod 26-27). The interior 'lovingly lined' (*ratsuf ahavah*) by the daughters of Jerusalem makes the bridal litter a collaborative act of the community's love. Purple dye was extracted from shellfish (*Murex*) at Tyre and Sidon and was the most expensive fabric color in the ancient world.
3.11: 'Come out, daughters of Zion, and gaze on King Solomon with the crown his mother crowned him on his wedding day' — the *atarah* (crown) used here is the wedding crown, distinct from the royal crown (*nezer*). Jewish tradition associated the wedding crown with joy; the Mishnah notes that wedding crowns were banned after the destruction of the temple as a sign of mourning. The phrase 'the day his heart rejoiced' (*simchat libo*) uses the heart (*lev*) for the seat of joy; the wedding day is the day of the heart's fullness — a statement about love's consummation as the highest human joy.

4.1: 'Your eyes behind your veil are doves' — the *wasf* (descriptive praise poem) of the bridegroom begins. The veil (*tzamah*, covering) was worn by women for honor and modesty in ancient Israelite society; the eyes visible through or above the veil heightened their perceived beauty. 'Hair like a flock of goats cascading down Gilead' — the movement of a flock of goats descending a hillside in the Gilead region (the Transjordanian highlands) was a familiar pastoral sight: flowing, dark, and in motion. The comparison describes the beloved's hair as simultaneously numerous, dark, and graceful.
4.2: 'Your teeth are like a flock of freshly shorn, washed sheep, each with its twin — not one missing' — the dental description is among the most unusual but reflects ANE standards: white, matched teeth (none missing) were a mark of health and beauty. The freshly washed sheep are brilliantly white; 'each with its twin' (each tooth with its opposite) = a full, matching set. The ANE world was without modern dentistry; a full set of matched white teeth was genuinely remarkable.
4.3: 'Your lips are like a scarlet thread, your mouth lovely; your cheeks like pomegranate halves behind your veil' — scarlet thread (*chut hashani*) was used to mark Rahab's window (Josh 2:18) and as part of the tabernacle furnishings (Exod 25:4); here it marks the beloved's lips with the same distinctive color. The pomegranate (*rimmon*) half's blush of rose-red through the white veil describes the rosy cheeks. Pomegranates appeared on the hem of the high priest's robe (Exod 28:33-34) and on the tops of the temple pillars (1 Kgs 7:20).
4.4: 'Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses of stone, a thousand shields hanging on it — all warriors' shields' — the tower of David (*migdal David*) was a known Jerusalem fortification, the kind of tower on which shields and weapons were displayed (Ezek 27:11 describes shields and helmets hung on Tyre's towers). The beloved's neck, adorned with multiple strands of jewelry, is compared to such a tower — strong, adorned, and marking military prestige. The thousand shields = the multiple rows of necklace strands that cover the neck like a tower's decorative armory.
4.5: 'Your two breasts are like two young fawns, twins of a gazelle, feeding among the lilies' — the twin fawns (*tefaché tze'vi*, young gazelles) are paired, delicate, and gentle in their movement. The gazelle fawn was a standard of tenderness and grace in ANE aesthetics. 'Feeding among the lilies' returns to the pastoral setting that recurs throughout the Song — beauty and gentleness located in the most abundant and fragrant natural setting.
4.6: 'Until the day breaks and shadows flee, I will go to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense' — myrrh (*mor*) and frankincense (*levonah*) were the two primary aromatic resins of the ancient sacrificial and cultic world; frankincense was the incense ingredient of the tabernacle (Exod 30:34) and appears in the NT as one of the magi's gifts (Matt 2:11). The beloved describes his beloved's body with the language of the most sacred aromatic substances — her presence is like the fragrance of the sanctuary.
4.7: 'You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no defect in you' — the word *mum* (blemish, defect) is the technical cultic term for the disqualifying flaw in a sacrificial animal: only animals *without blemish* (*tamim*) were acceptable (Lev 22:21; Num 6:14). The bridegroom describes the beloved using the language of sacrificial acceptability: she is not only beautiful but unblemished in the technical, cultic sense — the kind of perfection that makes her acceptable as an offering. The NT applies this standard to Christ (Heb 9:14: who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God).
4.8: 'Come with me from Lebanon, from the peak of Amana, from the summit of Senir and Hermon' — Amana (*Aamanah*) is the mountain range north of Damascus; Senir and Hermon were Amorite and Aramean names respectively for the same or adjacent peaks of the Anti-Lebanon range. These were the northern frontier mountains of Israel's sphere — beyond which lay the dangerous wilderness and the territory of foreign powers. The 'dens of lions and mountain haunts of leopards' were literal: large predators inhabited these highlands in antiquity. The summons is to come from danger to safety, from the wild frontier to the beloved's presence.
4.9: 'You have stolen my heart with one glance, with one jewel of your necklace' — the verb *libbavtini* (you have hearted me) is a denominative construction from *lev* (heart): your single glance has taken the heart out of him. In the honor-shame culture of the ancient near east, the power of a woman's glance was recognized and discussed — 'the evil eye' (a harmful glance) and the 'beautiful eye' had real social power. The 'one jewel' of the necklace = the power of a single element of the beloved's beauty to overwhelm.
4.10: 'How much better is your love than wine' — the comparison echoes and elevates 1:2 (where the woman said the beloved's love was better than wine); now the man says the same of her love. The reciprocity of the comparison marks the mutuality of the Song: both voices use the same terms, value the same things, desire the same person. Perfumes better than 'any spice' — the word for spice (*bosem*) covers a wide range of aromatic substances; no specific spice could match the beloved's fragrance.
4.11: 'Your lips drip with nectar; honey and milk are under your tongue; the fragrance of your garments is like Lebanon' — honey (*devash*) and milk (*chalav*) together are the standard description of the Promised Land (Exod 3:8; 33:3; Num 13:27; Deut 6:3) — the land's abundance expressed through its two most valued natural products. The beloved's speech and presence evoke the abundance of the Promised Land itself; she is, in the beloved's experience, what the land promised to Israel. The fragrance of Lebanon (*levanon*) = the cedars' resinous scent, the most powerful natural aromatic of the region.
4.12: 'My sister, my bride, is a locked garden, a sealed spring, a closed fountain' — the locked garden (*gan na'ul*) is among the most explicit ANE symbols for female virginity and exclusivity. The Egyptian wisdom text Insinger Papyrus uses garden/spring imagery for female virtue; Mesopotamian texts use the sealed/locked vessel metaphor. The three images (locked garden, sealed spring, closed fountain) repeat the same idea with increasing urgency: she is inaccessible, her resources reserved for one person. The *na'ul* (locked/bolted) garden requires a key — the beloved's exclusive access.
4.13: 'Your branches are an orchard of pomegranates with every choice fruit' — the *pardes* (orchard/paradise garden) is a Persian loanword (*pairi-daeza*, walled garden) that entered Hebrew from Akkadian; it appears three times in the OT (Song 4:13; Eccl 2:5; Neh 2:8) and becomes the source of the Greek *paradeisos* (paradise). The beloved's garden is stocked with the most valuable fruits and aromatic plants: pomegranates, henna, nard — all luxury items. The catalogue of spices reflects the international trade network that brought India, Arabia, and the Near East together in the spice trade.
4.14: Nard (*nerd*), saffron (*karkom*), calamus (*qaneh*), cinnamon (*qinnamon*), frankincense (*levonah*), myrrh (*mor*), and aloes (*ahalot*) — a remarkable concentration of luxury imports. Saffron was from the crocus of Cilicia and Persia; calamus and cinnamon came from India via Arabia; aloes (*ahalot*) were a fragrant wood from southeast Asia (the Indian *aguru* or eaglewood, Aquilaria species). The concentration of international spices in the Song reflects the Solomonic era's trade connections (1 Kgs 10:14-15; the spice trade through Arabia).
4.15: 'A garden fountain, a well of fresh water, streams flowing from Lebanon' — the sealed spring of v12 is now opened, or perhaps the sealed spring is contrasted with the bubbling fountain of her love once the garden is entered. Fresh water (*mayim chayyim*, living/running water) was the most valued commodity in the ancient near east, scarce and precious. The image of streams flowing down from Lebanon — the snow-fed mountain streams that brought life to the lowlands — makes the beloved the source of life-giving abundance.
4.16: 'Awake, O north wind; come, O south wind; blow through my garden' — the north and south winds in Israel had different characters: the north wind (*tzafon*) brought cold and rain; the south wind (*teiman*) brought warmth and sometimes the dry *sirocco* from the desert. Together they represent all winds — the beloved invites the full force of nature to carry her fragrance to the beloved. The invitation 'let my beloved come to his garden' shifts the possessive: the locked garden of v12 is now 'his garden' — she belongs to him; the invitation completes the transfer. The garden that was locked (v12) is now offered freely.

5.1: 'I have come to my garden, my sister, my bride' — the beloved accepts the invitation of 4:16; his arrival completes the movement of ch. 4 from sealed garden to open welcome. The gathering of myrrh, spice, honeycomb, honey, wine, and milk enacts the abundance promised in 4:13-15. The closing 'eat, friends; drink deeply, be drunk with love' — the wedding feast shared with companions; the banquet context (cf. 2:4) places the consummation within community celebration. The language of eating, drinking, and intoxication was standard in ANE descriptions of both feasting and sexual fulfillment.
5.2: 'I slept, but my heart was awake' — the second dream-vision begins; the heart (*lev*) watching while the body sleeps reflects the ANE understanding of the heart as the active, willing center of the person rather than the passive recipient of experience. The beloved's knock and voice (*qol*) is a literary device: we hear the words he would say in the dream, not the actual knock. The four address terms (*sister, darling, dove, perfect one*) accumulate into the fullest possible declaration of love. The 'head drenched with dew' locates him outside, in the night air — waiting.
5.3: 'I have taken off my robe — how can I put it back on?' — the *ketoneth* (robe, the basic garment worn next to the body) was taken off before sleeping. Re-dressing to open the door was not a trivial act; it also meant re-dirtying feet already washed for bed. The beloved's hesitation is humanly authentic — the gap between desire and the cost of action. In honor-shame terms, going to the door at night in a state of undress also carried social risk; the beloved weighs her ease against her desire and, in the dream, hesitates fatally.
5.4: 'My beloved thrust his hand through the latch opening' — the mechanical description of ancient door latches: a hand could be inserted through an opening to operate the latch from outside; the *chor* (hole, opening) in the door for the hand is a literal description. The beloved's act of attempting entry creates the *hamah* (intense longing, literally: 'my inward parts were troubled for him') in the woman — the same word used for the roar of surging waters (Ps 46:3; Isa 17:12) and the noise of a tumultuous crowd: an overwhelming physical sensation.
5.5: 'My fingers with flowing myrrh on the handles of the lock' — when the woman rises to open the door, her hands drip with *mor over* (flowing/liquid myrrh, the finest grade of myrrh as opposed to solid lumps). This may be the costly perfume that the beloved applied before the encounter, now transferred to the door — a fragrant trace of his presence. Myrrh was applied to the body and hair as luxury perfume; its presence on the door handles is both aromatic and evocative of the beloved's nearness.
5.6: 'I opened to my beloved, but he had gone' — the moment of missed connection; love's most painful experience. The silence that follows ('I called but he did not answer') is the divine hiddenness translated into the register of human love. Similar silences appear in Psalm 22:1-2 (my God, why have you forsaken me — I cry but you do not answer) and Isaiah 55:6 (seek the LORD while he may be found). The Song's dream-world reflects the actual experience of longing and absence that characterizes both human love and Israel's experience of the hidden God.
5.7: 'The guards found me, beat me, wounded me; the watchmen on the walls stripped off my shawl' — the contrast with the neutral encounter in 3:3 is stark. The same *shomerim* (watchmen/guards) who merely encountered her before now beat (*hikkuni*) and wound (*peshu*) her; the *shomerim homaot* (watchmen on the walls) are a different group. In the social world of the text, a woman found wandering alone at night in a disheveled state could be treated as a vagrant or a woman of ill repute; her *redid* (veil or shawl) is stripped — an act of social shaming, exposing what should be covered. The beloved risks public humiliation for love.
5.8: 'Tell him I am faint with love' — the woman's message to be conveyed via the daughters of Jerusalem: *holat ahavah ani* (I am sick with love). The lovesickness (*chalah me'ahavah*) is a medical state in ANE literature: Egyptian physicians described the physical symptoms of love's absence (racing pulse, listlessness, fever) as a genuine malady. The woman's willingness to broadcast her lovestruck state to strangers reflects the intensity that overrides the normal restraint of the honor-shame system.
5.9: 'What makes your beloved better than any other lover?' — the daughters of Jerusalem's challenge prompts the extended *wasf* of vv. 10-16. Their question is the standard competitive honor discourse of the ancient world: each person's beloved is supposed to be the best, but what is the specific *excellence* (*mah*) that distinguishes him? The title 'O most beautiful of women' (*hayafa bannashim*) repeats 1:8, confirming her pre-eminence as the context for the challenge.
5.10: 'My beloved is radiant and rugged, outstanding among ten thousand men' — *tzach ve'adom* (radiant/white and ruddy/red): the combination of fair skin and a healthy reddish complexion. David was described with similar terms (*admoni*, ruddy, with beautiful eyes, 1 Sam 16:12); Lamentations 4:7 describes the Nazirites as 'rudder than rubies, more radiant than sapphires.' *Dagul merevavah* (outstanding among ten thousand, literally: distinguished/bannered among myriads) — the beloved stands out from an army of men as a standard-bearer stands from the crowd.
5.11: 'His head is like purest gold; his hair wavy and dark as a raven' — gold (*paz*, the finest refined gold) for the head; *taltalim* (wavy, looping curls) for the hair, dark as *orev* (raven) — the raven's black was the standard of intense darkness in Hebrew (cf. Lam 4:8, where blackened skin signals deprivation; Prov 7:9, the dark of the night). The beloved's combination of golden (royally precious) head and raven-dark hair is a description of maximum contrast and richness.
5.12: 'His eyes are like doves beside streams of water — bathed in milk, perfectly set' — the dove-eye comparison from 1:15 is now applied by her to him; the mutuality of the imagery is characteristic. 'Bathed in milk' (*rachatzot bechalav*) = brilliantly white or perhaps watery/clear; 'perfectly set' (*yoshvot al milat*) may refer to the eye sitting perfectly in its socket, or to gems set in their bezels. The elaborate precision of the description reflects the ANE *wasf* tradition's cataloging of beauty.
5.13: 'His cheeks are like beds of spice, banks of sweet herbs; his lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh' — the cheeks (*lechaiv*) described as aromatic spice beds: fragrant and carefully cultivated. The lips (*sefatav*) dripping with flowing myrrh: the beloved's speech is as fragrant as the finest perfume; 'lilies' for the lips suggests softness and color. The aromatic body description follows the ANE convention of comparing the beloved's various features to luxury goods — spices, gold, ivory, precious stones.
5.14: 'His hands are gold cylinders set with jewels; his torso carved ivory overlaid with sapphires' — the gold *gliley zahav* (gold cylinders or rollings) set with *tarshish* (a gem, possibly beryl or chrysolite from Tarshish in Spain) describe massive, ringed forearms. The torso (*me'av*) described as carved ivory (*shen*) — elephant ivory was the luxury material par excellence in the ancient near east — overlaid (*muleefet*) with sapphires (*sappirim*; possibly lapis lazuli rather than modern sapphire). The description turns the human body into a work of art in precious materials.
5.15: 'His legs are like columns of alabaster, set in sockets of gold; his form like Lebanon, magnificent as the cedars' — alabaster (*shesh*) columns set in gold sockets describe the architectural grandeur of Solomon's palace and temple (1 Kgs 7:15-22); the beloved's legs are as strong and magnificent as those temple columns. 'Magnificent as the cedars' (*bechur ka'arazim*) — the cedar of Lebanon was the superlative of natural beauty and majesty; the beloved's overall form has the scale and grandeur of a cedar forest.
5.16: 'His mouth is sweetness itself; he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend' — *cheeko mamtaqim* (his mouth/palate is sweetnesses, plural of intensity) — the plural expresses excess of quality. The closing declaration 'this is my beloved and this is my friend' (*zeh dodi vezeh re'i*) is the climax of the *wasf*: after all the royal and artistic comparisons, the final word is not 'king' or 'warrior' but *dodi* (my beloved) and *re'i* (my friend). The integration of erotic love (*dod*) and friendship (*re'a*) is the Song's fullest statement of what ideal human love contains.

6.1: 'Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful of women?' — the daughters of Jerusalem, having heard the extended *wasf* of 5:10-16, are now won over: they want to help search. The title 'most beautiful of women' (*hayafa bannashim*) confirms the social recognition that was claimed in 1:8; the community's estimation of the beloved is changed by the beloved's own testimony about him. Their offer to search together reflects the community's role in supporting the love relationship.
6.2: 'My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and gather lilies' — the woman, in the very act of answering the search-question, realizes: she knows where he is. The garden (*ganno*) is her; he has gone to her. The spice beds and lily-grazing return to the pastoral imagery of 2:1-2 and 4:13-14. The woman's sudden realization that she is the garden provides the transition to the mutual-possession formula of v. 3.
6.3: 'I am my beloved's and he is mine; he grazes among the lilies' — the refrain of mutual possession restated; this is the second of three occurrences (2:16; 6:3; 7:10). The subtle change from 2:16 ('my beloved is mine and I am his') to 6:3 ('I am my beloved's and he is mine') marks a shift of emphasis: in 2:16 she asserts her claim on him first; in 6:3 his claim on her comes first. The variation is not random but reflects the deepening of the relationship through the trials of the intervening chapters.
6.4: 'You are beautiful as Tirzah, lovely as Jerusalem, awe-inspiring as an army under banners' — Tirzah (*Tirtzah*, 'pleasantness') was the capital of the northern kingdom before Omri built Samaria (1 Kgs 15:33; 16:17-18, 23-24); it was presumably as famous for beauty as Jerusalem was for grandeur. To compare the beloved to both capitals is to say she embodies all of Israel's beauty in both its northern and southern forms. *Nidgalot* (as armies with banners) — the military standard (*degel*) again; the beloved's beauty is simultaneously lovely and formidable, inspiring both desire and awe.
6.5: 'Look away — your eyes overwhelm me' — *hirhivuni* (they have made me restless, disturbed me) from *rahav* (to overwhelm, to be turbulent); the same root as Rahab, the chaos-monster of the deep (Ps 89:10; Isa 51:9). The beloved's eyes cause the same disruption in the lover that the divine glory causes: an overwhelming, destabilizing experience. The echo of Ezekiel's vision language and the Song's use of overwhelming beauty suggest a consciousness that creaturely beauty can produce something analogous to the religious experience of the divine presence.
6.6: The description of teeth (repeating 4:2) and cheeks (v. 7, repeating 4:3) — the repetition of elements from the first *wasf* is a literary device signaling the continuing and unchanging beauty of the beloved. In oral poetry, the repetition of formulaic descriptions reinforced the beauty's consistency across time and occasion; the beloved is as beautiful in this moment as she was at the beginning of the *wasf*-sequence.
6.7: 'Your cheeks are like pomegranate halves behind your veil' — repeating 4:3; the veil (*tzamah*) appears again, creating the partial visibility that heightens the beauty. The pomegranate's blush through the veil is one of the Song's most evocative images — the fruit's color visible through the fabric, suggesting beauty barely concealed.
6.8: 'There may be sixty queens and eighty concubines, and maidens beyond counting' — the numbers scale hierarchically: queens (*melachot*, 60), concubines (*pilegashim*, 80), and unlimited maidens (*almot ein mispar*). The harem scale invokes the Solomonic court tradition (1 Kgs 11:3 reports 700 wives and 300 concubines, numbers that represent legendary excess); whether literal or hyperbolic, the comparison establishes the context: amid all possible women, the beloved is unique.
6.9: 'My dove, my flawless one, is unique — her mother's only daughter, treasured by the one who bore her' — *achat hi* (she is the one, the unique one): the superlative of singularity. 'Only daughter' (*yechidah le'immah*) recalls Psalm 22:20 ('my only life'; *yechidati*) and Judges 11:34 (Jephthah's only daughter). Being the mother's only daughter and her beloved (*barah*) implies both preciousness and vulnerability. The social validation that follows — queens and concubines praising her — inverts the usual hierarchy: those of higher formal status acknowledge the superiority of the beloved.
6.10: 'Who is this who appears like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awe-inspiring as an army with banners?' — the fourth appearance of the *nidgalot* (army with banners) formula (cf. v.4). The sequence dawn/moon/sun scales upward in luminosity: the dawn announces the day; the moon is the night's full light; the sun is the supreme light-source. In the ancient near east, the celestial bodies had patron deities (sun = Shamash; moon = Sin; Venus/dawn = Ishtar); the Song borrows their luminosity while emptying them of divine status, applying the cosmic comparisons to a human beloved.
6.11: 'I went down to the grove of walnut trees to look at the new growth in the valley' — *ginnat egoz* (walnut grove) was an agricultural feature of the Palestinian landscape; the inspection of new growth (*yonqot* = new shoots; *hanetzu hagenafim* = whether the vines have budded; *pitchu harimonim* = whether the pomegranates have bloomed) reflects the agricultural calendar's rhythms. The lover, having praised the beloved to the heights of cosmic imagery, is here shown in a mundane scene of agricultural inspection — the movement from cosmic praise to ordinary life.
6.12: 'Before I realized it, my longing had placed me beside my noble kinsman's chariot' — *ammi-nadib* is one of the Song's most contested phrases: a proper name ('Amminadib'), a compound noun ('my people's noble one'), or a reference to the beloved himself. The sense seems to be: the woman's longing (*nefesh*) transported her, before she knew it, to the presence of the beloved's chariot. The sudden transportation of the *nefesh* (the whole self, the deep desire) is a metaphor for how overwhelming longing relocates a person — she was in the walnut grove; suddenly, mentally/emotionally, she was beside him.
6.13: 'Come back, come back, O Shulamite' — the only occurrence of the name 'Shulamite' (*hashulammit*) in the Song. The most likely derivation is from Shunem (*Shunam*) in the Jezreel Valley (cf. Abishag the Shunammite, 1 Kgs 1:3; the great woman of Shunem, 2 Kgs 4:8). The feminine suffix gives a gentilic meaning: the woman from Shunem. Alternatively, *Shulammit* is the feminine form of *Shlomo* (Solomon) — a mirror-name, suggesting the beloved and the lover are counterparts of the same name. The 'dance between two companies' (*machol hamachanayim*) may be a processional or battle dance where two groups face each other.
